Course Description

Fundamental concepts, techniques, and applications of risk assessment and risk

management for engineering systems; current industry practices for risk

assessment of industrial facilities using qualitative and quantitative approaches are

demonstrated through exercises and case studies from diverse engineering fields.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Graduate classification. Cross Listing: CHEN 660 and

SENG 660.

Special Course Designation

None

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Comprehend and articulate the fundamental goals and methodologies of the

Systems Engineering discipline.

Operate proficiently with international standards governing Systems

Engineering and Risk Management.

Conduct a comprehensive risk analysis, including the identification and

assessment of potential hazards, to develop effective mitigation strategies.

Develop and interpret probabilistic risk assessments for intricate engineering

systems.

Evaluate and model the reliability of individual engineering components and

complex, interconnected systems.

Grading Policy

Quizzes, homework, assignments (20%)

Unit 1 Evaluation: Systems Engineering (20%)

Unit 2 Evaluation: Risk Management (20%)

Unit 3 Evaluation: Probabilistic Risk Assessment - PRA (10%)

Unit 4 Evaluation: Reliability (30%)

[90%,100%] gets an A, [80%,90%) gets a B, [70%,80%) gets a C, [60%, 70%) gets a D,

and less than 60% gets an F.

Major evaluations will be administered at the end of each module. Minor tests and

homework will be given throughout the module. Minor assessment will account for

no more than 33% of the module's weight.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

No late work will be accepted.

Additional Course Information

Scientific Software

MATLAB, R, Excel, or Python might also be used for classwork and homework.

Course Format

Online lectures with exercises, quizzes, and evaluations.

Read the assigned material, including examples, and try to solve the exercises

in the handouts.

Watch the video lectures.

Review class exercises; optionally, complement your preparation by working

with the suggested textbooks.

Use the instructor's office hours.

Turn in quizzes; if anything is unclear, make a note of each. Partial credit will be

given when possible.

To contest a grade, please submit a formal written objection within three

days of the graded evaluation's due date.

If necessary, tests and exams might be regraded to ensure the integrity

and accuracy of the questions. Some students' grades may decrease

after the regrading.

Communications

To maintain course integrity, all communications must be conducted through Canvas

(via a message). The instructor will not respond to course-related questions via email

or chat.

How to Succeed in the Course?

Read and abide by the  Aggie Code of Honor

Be respectful of your peers and instructor.

Plan to spend 12 hours on the course each week, including time needed to

watch the video lectures.

Do not put things off to the last minute; this course needs extreme diligence.

Ask questions using Canvas.

Solve the exercise problems on your own before looking at the solution (when

applicable).

If something is not clear, ask questions during office hours (ask for help as early

as possible).

Attempt all the exercise problems before coming to class.

Prepare extremely well for quizzes and exams.

For the math-based units or modules, the procedure to obtain an answer is as

valid as the answer itself.
